About.title: What is this website for?
About.description: The <aip>Maltese Aeronautical Information Publication AIP</aip> is published by Malta Air Traffic Services (MATS); access to the official portal may require registration. This website aims to simplify the search for airfields, as the official AIP does not include a search function. We do not accept any liability for the accuracy or timeliness of the AIP (Aeronautical Information Publication), as it is not published by us. We only provide convenient links to the respective approach charts.

Weather.title: Weather
Weather.nearest: Weather from the nearest airport {station} ({km} km away)
Weather.hoursAdvisory: Advisory - confirm via NOTAM / current AIP before flight.
Weather.hoursNone: No operating hours on file. Many small VFR fields and heliports publish none, or only on request (O/R), home base (HO) or by NOTAM - always check the current AIP and NOTAMs before flight.
Weather.hoursOcrDisclaimer: Operating hours read automatically from the AIP page image by text recognition - may contain read errors. Verify against the current AIP before flight.
Weather.aipTextCaveat: Read automatically from the official AIP page image by text recognition (OCR) and may contain reading errors. Always verify against the current AIP before flight.

Common.homeLink: AIP:Aero - Aeronautical Information Publications (AIP) & approach charts for Europe
Common.installHint: Install as an app: open your browser's Share menu and choose 'Add to Home Screen'. Then open the app once while online and save your fields there again - the app has its own offline storage.
Common.installHintMac: On a Mac: install via Safari's Share menu using 'Add to Dock'. Then open the app once while online and save your fields there again - the app has its own offline storage.
Common.bulkNoSpace: Not enough free storage on this device
Common.bulkScope: Included: all airfield pages with aerodrome data. Not included: approach chart PDFs (can be saved per airfield) and live weather (needs a connection).
Common.favoritesEmpty: No favorites yet - save an airfield offline on its detail page and it will appear here.
Common.recentlyViewedEmpty: Airfields you open will appear here automatically for quick access.
Common.aipLoginHint: A login or registration may be required.
Common.noChartPdf: No separate chart PDF is published for this aerodrome - the AIP button above opens its official aerodrome entry.
Common.reportProblemTitle: Report incorrect or missing data for this aerodrome

TermsPage.title: Terms of Service
TermsPage.intro: AIP:Aero is a free search and index service for Aeronautical Information Publications (AIP), approach charts and airport data across Europe.
TermsPage.servicesText: <home>AIP:Aero</home> lets you find aerodromes by name or ICAO code and takes you directly to the official AIP publication of the respective country. In addition, our airport pages embed supplementary information such as weather reports (METAR/TAF), aerodrome data (runways, frequencies, elevation), maps and contact details, and pages can be saved for offline use. The service is provided free of charge and without registration.
TermsPage.disclaimerText: AIP:Aero cannot be held liable under any circumstances. We do not publish aeronautical documents ourselves: we only link to the latest Aeronautical Information Publication (AIP) made available by the official provider of the respective country. The same applies to weather data (METAR/TAF) and airport data: we embed this information on our pages, but it originates from the external sources listed below. We assume no liability or warranty for the accuracy, completeness or timeliness of any linked or embedded content, nor for the availability of this website. This website does not replace the official pre-flight briefing: the pilot in command remains solely responsible for obtaining and verifying all information required for a flight.
TermsPage.sourcesIntro: We obtain the linked and embedded data from the following sources (addresses shown as plain text, deliberately not clickable):
TermsPage.sourcesAip: Official AIP publications we link to
TermsPage.srcOpenAip: extended aerodrome data: runways, frequencies, fuel
TermsPage.metaDescription: Terms of service of AIP:Aero, the free AIP and approach chart search for Malta and Europe: service description, disclaimer of liability and data sources.

EfbPage.title: AIP:Aero pilot tools: EFB app, offline charts, imports and data
EfbPage.intro: How to install AIP:Aero on your EFB (Electronic Flight Bag) tablet, save approach charts offline, download country packs and hand fields over to your planning tools - step by step.
EfbPage.metaTitle: EFB Guide Malta - AIP & approach charts on your tablet | AIP:Aero
EfbPage.metaDescription: Install AIP:Aero as an app on your EFB tablet, save approach charts offline, download whole countries and import chart PDFs into ForeFlight, SkyDemon & Co (Malta).
EfbPage.installText: AIP:Aero is an installable web app: it opens full-screen like a native app and everything you save is available offline. No app store needed.
EfbPage.installIos: iPad / iPhone (Safari): open aip.aero, tap the share icon, then "Add to Home Screen".
EfbPage.installAndroid: Android (Chrome): open aip.aero, tap the menu, then "Add to home screen" / "Install app".
EfbPage.installDesktop: Desktop (Chrome/Edge): click the install icon in the address bar - or use the button below. macOS (Safari): Share icon, then "Add to Dock".
EfbPage.offlineText: Every airport detail page has a "Save offline" button. It stores the page and, where the AIP publishes one, the approach-chart PDF on your device; the field then appears under Favorites on the country page. Saved copies show their download date.
EfbPage.bulkText: On the <list>airport list</list> you can download all of a country's detail pages in one go (pages only, without chart PDFs - save those per field). The pack shows its size before downloading and can be updated or removed at any time.
EfbPage.favoritesText: Fields you save for offline use automatically appear as favorites, and the last airfields you opened are listed under "Recently viewed" - both are stored only on your device, no account needed. You will find both lists <country>on the country page</country>.
EfbPage.importTitle: Chart PDFs in ForeFlight, SkyDemon & Co.
EfbPage.importText: Where the national AIP publishes chart PDFs, the chart box on the detail page links the current PDF including its AIRAC date. Download it and import it into your EFB app as custom content, e.g. ForeFlight (Custom Content), SkyDemon (Custom Airfield Plates) or Garmin Pilot. Germany publishes HTML pages instead of PDFs - use the AIP link there.
EfbPage.toolsText: The location box on every detail page opens the same field directly in SkyVector, Windy and autorouter - handy for route planning, weather models and flight-plan filing.
EfbPage.weatherText: Detail pages show live METAR/TAF with plain-language decoding, the flight-category badge and a per-runway head- and crosswind breakdown - a quick cross-check, not a substitute for your official briefing.
EfbPage.customsText: Detail pages show whether a field offers customs clearance; where a national AIP lists it in GEN 1.2, the value is verified against that official source. For border-crossing paperwork the location box links the official forms (e.g. the UK General Aviation Report), and the map can filter for customs airfields. Rules change - always confirm with the current AIP and the local authority.
EfbPage.disclaimerText: AIP:Aero links official AIP publications but is not an official source. Offline copies are marked with their download date and can become outdated with every AIRAC cycle - verify chart currency before every flight and always obtain an official pre-flight briefing.
EfbPage.apiText: For developers and EFB providers: AIP:Aero's structured aerodrome data (the normalized aerodrome index, chart-PDF deep links, AIRAC cycles, coordinates, runways, frequencies and customs information) is available via API for integrations. We are open for cooperations; <contact>get in touch</contact>.
EfbPage.faq1a: Saved airfields and downloaded country packs work without internet; every offline copy shows a banner with its save date. Live weather (METAR/TAF) always needs a connection.
EfbPage.faq2a: All countries currently live are listed on the <home>homepage</home> - the coverage grows continuously. Each country card shows the available chart types.
EfbPage.faq3a: Chart links point to the official national AIP publications, including the AIRAC date where published. Always verify against the official source before flight.

CountryFaq.title: Frequently asked questions
CountryFaq.a1: The <glossary>AIP</glossary> (Aeronautical Information Publication) is the official aeronautical manual of Malta, published by Malta Air Traffic Services (MATS): aerodrome data, procedures and approach charts, amended in the 28-day <guides>AIRAC</guides> cycle. AIP:Aero links you straight to the official publication.
CountryFaq.a2: AIP:Aero is free: no account, no subscription. MATS publishes the AIP of Malta including <list>approach charts</list>; access to the official portal may require registration.
CountryFaq.q3: Which charts and airfields are available for Malta?
CountryFaq.a3: For Malta you will find <vfr>VFR airfields</vfr> such as Malta International Airport (LMML) - each with a direct link to the official chart, decoded weather (METAR/TAF) and aerodrome data.
CountryFaq.q4: Can I use AIP:Aero offline or on my EFB?
CountryFaq.a4: Yes - AIP:Aero installs as an app (PWA), and single aerodromes or the whole country can be saved offline. See the <efb>EFB guide</efb>.
CountryFaq.q5: Where can I learn to read a chart or decode METAR/TAF?
CountryFaq.a5: New to aeronautical charts? The <guides>pilot guides</guides> walk through reading a VFR/IFR approach chart, the AIRAC cycle and decoding METAR and TAF, and the <glossary>aviation glossary</glossary> explains the key terms like AIP, VFR/IFR, ICAO codes and chart types.

GlossaryPage.metaTitle: Aviation Glossary - AIP, AIRAC, VFR, METAR and More
GlossaryPage.metaDescription: Plain-language definitions of the key aeronautical terms: AIP and eAIP, the AIRAC cycle, VFR and IFR, METAR and TAF, ICAO codes and approach charts.
GlossaryPage.title: Aviation glossary
GlossaryPage.intro: Short, plain-language definitions of the aeronautical terms you meet across AIP:Aero, from the AIP and the AIRAC cycle to METAR, TAF and approach charts.

aip.name: AIP (Aeronautical Information Publication)
aip.def: The official manual published by a country's aviation authority containing all information needed for flight, including aerodrome data, airspace, procedures and approach charts. AIP:Aero links every chart directly to its national AIP, always the authoritative source.

eaip.name: eAIP (electronic AIP)
eaip.def: The web-based, hyperlinked version of the AIP that most European authorities now publish. Its standard EUROCONTROL layout is what the AIP:Aero crawlers read to build each country's airport index.

airac.name: AIRAC cycle
airac.def: The fixed 28-day schedule on which aeronautical data changes take effect worldwide, so pilots everywhere update their charts on the same date. Every airport page on AIP:Aero shows the AIRAC edition of the linked chart so you can confirm it is current.

vfr.name: VFR (Visual Flight Rules)
vfr.def: The rules for flying by outside visual reference in good weather, used by most general-aviation and recreational pilots. AIP:Aero lists VFR aerodromes and their visual approach charts for each country.

ifr.name: IFR (Instrument Flight Rules)
ifr.def: The rules for flying solely by instruments and air-traffic-control clearances, in cloud or poor visibility. IFR airports carry instrument approach charts (IAC) for precise, procedure-based approaches.

metar.name: METAR
metar.def: A coded, twice-hourly observation of the actual weather at an aerodrome: wind, visibility, cloud, temperature and pressure. AIP:Aero decodes the latest METAR into plain language on every airport page that has a reporting station.

taf.name: TAF (Terminal Aerodrome Forecast)
taf.def: A coded weather forecast for the area around an aerodrome, typically valid for 24 to 30 hours. It uses the same abbreviations as the METAR and is decoded alongside it on AIP:Aero.

icao.name: ICAO location indicator
icao.def: The four-letter code that uniquely identifies an aerodrome worldwide (for example EDDF for Frankfurt), assigned by the International Civil Aviation Organization. AIP:Aero addresses every airport page by its ICAO code.

charts.name: Approach and aerodrome charts (VAC, IAC, ADC)
charts.def: The official diagrams pilots use to arrive at and move around an aerodrome: the Visual Approach Chart (VAC) for VFR, the Instrument Approach Chart (IAC) for IFR, and the Aerodrome Chart (ADC) for the ground layout. AIP:Aero links the exact chart PDF wherever the national AIP publishes one.

operatinghours.name: Operating hours (UTC)
operatinghours.def: Aerodrome operating hours in the AIP (AD 2.3) are given in UTC (Zulu time). When daylight saving affects them, the AIP marks it with a bracketed footnote - for example '0730-1600 (0630-1500)', where the bracketed values apply during the summer period - or a note such as '-1 HR during summer time'. The base value stays UTC. AIP:Aero shows the season currently in effect, always in UTC.

runwaydistances.name: Runway distances (physical length vs TORA, TODA, ASDA, LDA)
runwaydistances.def:
The runway length AIP:Aero shows is the PHYSICAL runway dimension, not the declared distances a pilot uses for performance (the AIP publishes these per runway direction in AD 2.13):
  • Physical length - the full length of the strip; do not use it for take-off or landing performance.
  • TORA (Take-Off Run Available) - the run available for the take-off roll.
  • TODA (Take-Off Distance Available) - TORA plus any clearway.
  • ASDA (Accelerate-Stop Distance Available) - TORA plus any stopway.
  • LDA (Landing Distance Available) - measured from the threshold; can be shorter than the physical length when the threshold is displaced.
Always base take-off and landing performance on the declared distances in the current AIP, never on the physical length.

GuidesPage.metaTitle: Pilot Guides - Reading Charts, the AIRAC Cycle, Decoding METAR/TAF
GuidesPage.metaDescription: Short, practical guides for private pilots: how to read an approach chart, how the AIRAC cycle works, and how to decode METAR and TAF weather reports.
GuidesPage.title: Pilot guides
GuidesPage.intro: Short, practical guides to the aeronautical documents and data you use on AIP:Aero, written for private pilots. Each one links back to the airport pages where you can put it into practice.

charts.title: How to read an approach chart
charts.intro: An approach chart packs an aerodrome's arrival and departure procedures into one sheet. Read it top to bottom before you fly.
charts.s1: Check the header first: the aerodrome name, ICAO code, chart type and the AIRAC effective date. AIP:Aero shows the AIRAC edition on every airport page so you can confirm the chart is current.
charts.s2: Read the plan view, the bird's-eye layout of runways, navaids, holding patterns and airspace, with tracks and distances between the fixes.
charts.s3: Read the profile view and the minima: the descent profile, the altitude at each fix, and the decision altitude or minimum descent altitude for the approach.
charts.s4: Note the missed-approach procedure and the communication and lighting boxes, so you know the go-around track and the frequencies before you need them.

airac.title: Understanding the AIRAC cycle
airac.intro: AIRAC is the fixed calendar that keeps the world's aeronautical data in step, so charts change on predictable dates rather than at random.
airac.s1: Changes take effect every 28 days, on the same date worldwide. There are 13 cycles a year, each identified by year and number (for example 2026-07).
airac.s2: A new edition is published well before its effective date so you can prepare, but it only becomes current on that date. Always fly the edition in force.
airac.s3: On AIP:Aero every airport page shows the AIRAC edition of the linked chart, so you can check at a glance whether you are looking at the current cycle.

metar.title: Decoding METAR and TAF
metar.intro: METAR is the current observed weather at an aerodrome; TAF is the forecast for the area around it. Both use the same compact code.
metar.s1: Read the groups in order: station, day and time, wind (direction and speed), visibility, weather and cloud, then temperature and dew point and the QNH pressure.
metar.s2: The flight category sums it up: VFR, MVFR, IFR or LIFR, based on the ceiling and visibility, so you can judge suitability at a glance.
metar.s3: AIP:Aero decodes the latest METAR and TAF into plain language on every airport page that has a reporting station, with the raw report kept alongside.

faq.q1: Do I still need the official AIP if I use AIP:Aero?
faq.a1: Yes. AIP:Aero links the official national AIP and charts and adds context, but the national AIP is always the authoritative source, so check it before every flight.
faq.q2: How do I know a chart is current?
faq.a2: Every airport page shows the AIRAC edition of the linked chart. Compare it with the AIRAC cycle in force on your flight date.
faq.q3: Where can I try this out?
faq.a3: Open any airport page from the airport list for your country and you will find the chart, the decoded weather and the aerodrome data described in these guides.
